name: Spodumene

photos.locality: Tanco Mine, Bernic Lake, Manitoba, Canada
Pala Chief mine, San Diego County, California — gem-grade kunzite variety

identification.colour: White to grey; gem varieties: pale green (hiddenite), lavender to pink (kunzite)
identification.lustre: Vitreous to pearly on cleavage faces
identification.crystal_habit: Prismatic, typically flattened; strongly striated lengthwise along c-axis; often coarse and massive in ore deposits

canada.geological_setting: Found exclusively in lithium-caesium-tantalum (LCT) pegmatites — late-crystallising granitic bodies enriched in incompatible elements. Canada's most significant resources are at Patriot Battery Metals' Shaakichiuwaanaan deposit in Quebec's James Bay territory (one of the largest hard-rock lithium resources in the Americas), the Yellowknife pegmatite province in the Northwest Territories, and emerging projects near Val-d'Or, QC. British Columbia hosts spodumene in the Shuswap Metamorphic Complex.

handling: No significant chemical hazards. As with all silicate minerals, avoid prolonged inhalation of fine dust. Crystals are brittle on two well-developed cleavage planes; handle with care to avoid sharp edges.
known_for: The primary ore mineral driving the global EV battery revolution. Spodumene concentrate is processed into lithium hydroxide for cathode manufacture. Canada's James Bay spodumene resources are positioned to serve North American gigafactories under the Canada-US Critical Minerals Agreement. The mineral was first described in 1800 from a Swedish pegmatite; its economic importance was negligible until the electrification era.
gem_note: Kunzite (pink to lilac) and hiddenite (emerald-green) are highly sought gem varieties. Both display strong trichroism — colour shifts dramatically depending on viewing angle. Kunzite crystals from large pegmatites can exceed 1,000 carats in cut weight. The colour of kunzite fades with prolonged UV exposure.

name: Uraninite

identification.colour: Pitch-black to grey-black; streak: black to brownish-black
identification.lustre: Greasy to submetallic; massive pitchblende form is dull

hardness.plain: Moderately hard — will scratch glass with effort

identification.crystal_habit: Cubic crystals (rare); most commonly massive, botryoidal, or colloform (pitchblende form)

canada.geological_setting: In Saskatchewan's Athabasca Basin, uraninite occurs at unconformity-type deposits where uranium-bearing hydrothermal fluids were focused along the contact between Proterozoic sandstone and older basement rocks. McArthur River, Cigar Lake, and Key Lake have ore grades 10–100× higher than any other deposit type in the world. In Ontario's Elliot Lake and Bancroft areas, uraninite occurs in Archean conglomerate reefs and pegmatites.

handling: RADIOACTIVE — uraninite emits alpha, beta, and gamma radiation. Handle only with proper radiation safety protocols: minimise exposure time, maximise distance, use shielding. Do not handle without training. Specimens intended for display should be stored in sealed containers. Radon gas, a decay product, can accumulate in enclosed spaces. Consult your jurisdiction's radiation safety guidelines before acquiring specimens.
known_for: The primary ore mineral of uranium — the fuel for nuclear power and a feedstock for defence applications. Saskatchewan's Athabasca Basin is the world's highest-grade uranium jurisdiction, accounting for roughly 22% of global primary uranium production. The mineral pitchblende (massive uraninite) was studied by Marie and Pierre Curie, who discovered radium and polonium within it in 1898. Canada was the world's largest uranium producer for most of the 20th century.

name: Cobaltite

identification.colour: Silver-white to greyish-white, often with a reddish tinge; tarnishes to grey

hardness.plain: Moderately hard — roughly the hardness of a steel knife blade

identification.crystal_habit: Cubic, pyritohedric, or octahedral crystals; also massive and granular

canada.geological_setting: In Ontario's Cobalt camp (Temiskaming Highlands), cobaltite occurs in five-element vein deposits — a distinctive hydrothermal deposit type containing cobalt, nickel, arsenic, silver, and bismuth minerals in carbonate veins cross-cutting Proterozoic diabase sills and Archean metasediments. The camp produced over 600 million troy ounces of silver between 1903 and the 1980s. Modern interest focuses on processing historic tailings for battery-grade cobalt and refinery development at Temiskaming Shores.

handling: Contains arsenic — a cumulative toxin. Do not grind, heat, or generate dust without ventilation and respiratory protection. Wash hands thoroughly after handling. Specimens with visible pink erythrite (cobalt bloom) surface oxidation should be treated with particular care, as erythrite is more soluble than cobaltite. Store away from food and children.
known_for: The mineral that gave Cobalt, Ontario its name and made it one of the richest mining camps in North American history. The silver boom of the early 1900s built the Canadian banking industry and financed expansion of northern Ontario's rail network. Today cobalt is strategic for lithium-ion battery cathodes (NMC and NCA chemistry), and Canada is working to reactivate the Cobalt camp's refining infrastructure.

name: Pentlandite

identification.colour: Bronze-yellow to light bronze-brown; streak: light bronze-brown

hardness.plain: Relatively soft — a copper coin will scratch it

identification.crystal_habit: Massive; rarely as distinct grains; commonly intergrown with pyrrhotite as characteristic flame-textured lamellae visible in polished section

canada.geological_setting: Occurs in magmatic nickel-copper-PGE sulphide deposits associated with mafic and ultramafic intrusions. The Sudbury Basin (Ontario) — formed by a 1.85 billion-year-old meteorite impact that melted and differentiated crustal rocks — hosts one of the world's largest nickel-copper sulphide deposits. Vale's Sudbury operations and Glencore's Sudbury Integrated Nickel Operations produce nickel, copper, cobalt, and platinum-group elements. The Thompson Nickel Belt in Manitoba is a world-class magmatic system hosted in Proterozoic komatiites.

handling: No significant chemical hazards under normal handling. Fine dust should not be inhaled (general mineral precaution). Note that pentlandite is commonly associated with pyrrhotite, which on oxidation can generate sulphuric acid in mine drainage contexts.
known_for: The primary ore mineral of nickel — one of Canada's most economically significant metals. Sudbury's nickel has been mined continuously since 1883 and continues to supply global stainless steel, battery, and aerospace markets. Nickel's role in high-density lithium-ion cathodes (NMC 811 chemistry) has renewed global demand. Canada is among the few countries with large-scale Class 1 nickel refining capacity outside China.

name: Graphite

photos.locality: Minas Gerais, Brazil — large crystalline flakes

identification.colour: Steel-grey to black; streak: black, shiny and smears
identification.lustre: Metallic to dull; distinctly greasy feel

hardness.plain: Very soft — marks paper like a pencil (it is the pencil)

identification.crystal_habit: Platy hexagonal flakes (flake graphite); massive (amorphous graphite); vein or lump graphite in metamorphic veins

canada.geological_setting: Canadian graphite occurs mainly as crystalline flake graphite in Precambrian marbles, gneisses, and schists formed by metamorphism of organic carbon-rich sediments. Key deposits include Zenyatta's Albany graphite in a nepheline syenite pipe (northern Ontario) and Nouveau Monde Graphite's Matawinie project near Montreal, QC — one of the largest natural flake graphite resources in the Americas. Quebec's Outaouais region hosts additional advanced-stage projects targeting battery anode markets.

handling: No significant toxicological hazard. Carbon dust is a nuisance dust; avoid prolonged inhalation of fine graphite powder in industrial settings. The greasy feel is characteristic and will transfer to hands and surfaces. Natural graphite is chemically inert and safe to handle without PPE for specimen purposes.
known_for: The only critical mineral that also writes. Natural flake graphite is the primary anode material in lithium-ion batteries — every EV cell contains approximately 10× more graphite than lithium by weight. Canada's flake graphite deposits are among the highest-purity natural resources globally, positioned to supply the North American battery supply chain. Graphite also moderates nuclear reactors, lubricates machinery at extreme temperatures, and is the structural basis of carbon fibre and graphene.

name: Magnetite

identification.colour: Black to brownish black; streak: black
identification.lustre: Metallic to submetallic; earthy when massive

hardness.plain: Hard — will scratch glass; similar hardness to a pocket knife blade

identification.crystal_habit: Octahedral crystals (classic lodestone form); also massive granular and as disseminated grains or sand

canada.geological_setting: Magnetite is ubiquitous in Canadian geology. As a primary iron ore mineral it occurs in Precambrian banded iron formations (BIFs) across Quebec's Labrador Trough and Ontario's Michipicoten Belt. It is a by-product in virtually every Canadian base metal mine — disseminated in sulphide ore and as skarn replacements at BC copper-gold porphyries. The Ring of Fire (Ontario) hosts magnetite in chromite-bearing dunite and peridotite. The Lac Doré complex in Quebec carries vanadium-bearing magnetite of potential importance for vanadium redox flow batteries.

handling: No significant toxicological hazards. Strongly magnetic — keep away from electronic devices, credit cards, and precision instruments. Powdered magnetite is a fine dust; avoid prolonged inhalation.
known_for: The original compass mineral — naturally magnetised specimens (lodestone) guided navigation for over 2,000 years before the manufactured compass. Magnetite is Canada's primary iron ore mineral in BIF-hosted deposits and a critical by-product recovered from almost every sulphide mine. Vanadium-bearing magnetite at Lac Doré, QC is a future source of vanadium for high-strength alloy steel and grid-scale vanadium redox flow batteries.

name: Molybdenite

identification.lustre: Metallic; greasy feel on cleavage faces

hardness.plain: Very soft — leaves a greasy grey mark on paper; feels like graphite between fingers

identification.crystal_habit: Hexagonal platy or foliated masses; often as isolated flakes or rosettes in quartz veins and porphyry stockworks

canada.geological_setting: Molybdenite is Canada's primary source of molybdenum, on the federal critical minerals list. The most significant occurrences are in British Columbia's porphyry copper-molybdenum deposits — the Endako mine (near Fraser Lake), Highland Valley Copper (by-product), and the Kitsault deposit near Alice Arm. In the Yukon, the Casino porphyry system hosts world-scale copper-gold-molybdenum resources. Ontario's Renfrew County hosts quartz-molybdenite veins in Grenville-age gneisses.

handling: Low acute toxicity under normal handling. Molybdenum is an essential trace nutrient, but MoS₂ dust should not be inhaled in quantity. The extreme lubricity means surfaces become slippery when contaminated — exercise care on drill floors and near processing equipment.
known_for: The world's best natural dry lubricant — MoS₂ platelets slide on each other at temperatures where oil would burn away, making molybdenite grease standard in aerospace fasteners, firearms, and high-temperature industrial machinery. Molybdenum alloyed into steel at 0.25–8% dramatically increases strength-to-weight ratio and corrosion resistance — essential for pipeline steel, turbine blades, and high-speed cutting tools. Canada has historically been a global top-5 molybdenum producer.

name: Gold

photos.locality: Eagle's Nest Mine, Tuolumne County, California, USA

identification.colour: Yellow to pale yellow (alloyed with silver); streak: yellow (diagnostic for native metal)

hardness.plain: Very soft for a metal — a copper coin will scratch it; highly malleable and can be pounded into thin leaf

identification.crystal_habit: Octahedral, cubic, or dodecahedral crystals; more commonly as irregular grains, wires, dendrites, or nuggets; placer gold as rounded flakes and nuggets from stream transport

canada.geological_setting: Canada is among the world's largest gold producers. Ontario's Abitibi Greenstone Belt hosts world-class orogenic lode-gold deposits — Kirkland Lake, Timmins (Porcupine camp), and Red Lake — where gold occurs in quartz-carbonate veins and sulphide replacement zones in Archean mafic volcanics and tonalites. Quebec's Windfall Lake and Urban-Barry camp are among the most significant new discoveries. The Yukon's Klondike produced over 20 million troy ounces in the 1898 gold rush from Cenozoic placer gravels. British Columbia hosts porphyry-gold and epithermal systems at Brucejack and Red Chris.

handling: No toxicological hazards — metallic gold is chemically inert and safe to handle. Note that gold deposits frequently contain arsenic sulphides (arsenopyrite) as co-associated minerals. Gold specimens in arsenopyrite matrix should be treated as arsenic-bearing material and handled accordingly.
known_for: Canada's most storied metal. The 1898 Klondike gold rush drew over 100,000 prospectors to the Yukon, shaped Canadian confederation narratives, and built infrastructure across the north. Today Canada produces roughly 200 tonnes of gold annually — among the top 5 globally — with Ontario and Quebec as the leading provinces. Gold's extraordinary conductivity and corrosion resistance make it essential in electronics: every smartphone contains approximately 0.03 grams.

name: Galena

photos.locality: Sweetwater Mine, Reynolds County, Missouri, USA

identification.lustre: Metallic, brilliant on fresh surfaces; tarnishes to dull grey

hardness.plain: Soft — a copper coin will scratch it; cleaves perfectly into cubes

identification.crystal_habit: Cubic crystals, often as cubes and cubo-octahedra; perfect cubic cleavage in three directions gives characteristic stepped fracture surfaces; massive granular common in ores

canada.geological_setting: Galena is the primary ore mineral of lead and a major silver host (silver substitutes into galena's crystal lattice — argentiferous galena can carry hundreds of grams of silver per tonne). Canada's primary galena districts include SEDEX deposits of British Columbia (the Sullivan mine at Kimberley was the world's largest Pb-Zn mine until 2001), VMS deposits in New Brunswick (Caribou / Brunswick No. 12) and Newfoundland, and the Anvil Range silver-lead-zinc district of the Yukon.

handling: LEAD HAZARD — galena is a lead mineral. Lead is a cumulative neurotoxin with no safe blood level. Do not grind or generate dust without respiratory protection. Wash hands thoroughly after handling. Do not handle near food or around children. Specimens with white anglesite or yellow-brown cerussite surface oxidation may release lead more readily — treat with extra care.
known_for: The densest common mineral you will hold by hand — a palm-sized specimen weighs surprisingly heavy. Galena is the primary lead ore for 4,000 years of human history: Roman plumbing, medieval roofing, printing type, toxic paint, and now sealed lead-acid batteries. The silvery blue-grey cubic cleavage faces are unmistakeable and among the most photogenic of ore minerals. Canada's lead-zinc mining tradition built industrial northern communities from Kimberley, BC to Bathurst, NB.

name: Sphalerite

identification.colour: Highly variable: yellow, orange, red, brown, black (iron-rich), or colourless (rare); streak: pale yellow to brown
identification.lustre: Resinous to adamantine; strongly resinous in iron-bearing varieties

hardness.plain: Moderate — slightly harder than a copper coin; perfect dodecahedral cleavage in six directions

identification.crystal_habit: Tetrahedral or complex forms; commonly massive granular in ore deposits; characteristic curved resinous fracture when broken

canada.geological_setting: Sphalerite is Canada's primary zinc mineral and occurs across all major sulphide deposit types. The Sullivan deposit (BC) and the Flin Flon VMS camp (Manitoba-Saskatchewan border) produced zinc for over a century. New Brunswick's Brunswick No. 12 was one of the world's largest VMS zinc-lead-silver mines. Sphalerite in BC carbonate-hosted deposits and skarn systems also carries trace indium — a critical mineral for thin-film solar panels and LCD screens — making zinc refinery residues an important secondary indium source.

handling: Generally low hazard under normal handling. Pure ZnS is non-toxic; however, sphalerite in ore contexts may be associated with arsenic minerals. Cadmium is a trace constituent of some sphalerite and is toxic — treat ore concentrates with appropriate PPE. Avoid dust inhalation.
known_for: Every galvanized metal product — from highway guardrails to roofing to paperclip coatings — uses zinc derived primarily from sphalerite. Canada has been a top-5 global zinc producer for most of the 20th century. Sphalerite also concentrates critical trace elements: indium (for thin-film solar and touchscreens) and germanium (for fibre-optic cables and infrared optics) are almost entirely sourced as by-products of zinc smelting.
gem_note: Colourless to pale yellow gem-grade sphalerite (cleiophane) has a dispersion higher than diamond — it displays intense fire when faceted. Cut stones are too soft for jewellery wear but are prized by collectors. Honey-orange crystals from Spain's Picos de Europa are considered among the finest mineral specimens in existence.

name: Chromite

identification.colour: Black to brownish-black; streak: brown to dark brown (distinguishes from magnetite, which has black streak)

hardness.plain: Moderately hard — comparable to a knife blade; slightly magnetic in some specimens

identification.crystal_habit: Octahedral crystals; usually massive, granular, or as disseminated grains in ultramafic host rock

canada.geological_setting: Chromite forms exclusively in mafic and ultramafic igneous rocks — dunites, peridotites, and their serpentinised equivalents. Ontario's James Bay Lowlands host the Ring of Fire chromite deposits (discovered 2008), representing one of the world's largest undeveloped chromite resources. The primary deposits — Black Horse, Blackbird, and Big Daddy — are hosted in Archean dunite pods and komatiite sequences. Development requires a north-south access corridor and ongoing First Nations consultation and consent processes.

handling: No significant hazard for chromite ore (Cr³⁺). However, processing that oxidises chromite to hexavalent chromium (Cr⁶⁺) — such as high-temperature smelting — produces a known carcinogen. For specimen handling, apply normal mineral precautions: avoid dust inhalation; wash hands after contact.
known_for: Every piece of stainless steel contains chromium from chromite — typically 10–30% by weight. Canada's Ring of Fire holds one of the world's few remaining large undeveloped chromite resources outside South Africa and Kazakhstan, which together control over 80% of global supply. Ring of Fire development is a federal strategic priority but faces significant infrastructure, permitting, and Indigenous rights challenges that have delayed development since discovery.

name: Columbite-Tantalite

identification.colour: Black to brownish-black; streak: dark red to black

hardness.plain: Hard — will scratch glass; similar hardness to feldspar

identification.crystal_habit: Short prismatic to tabular crystals, often striated; also massive; forms a complete solid-solution series from columbite (Nb-dominant) to tantalite (Ta-dominant)

canada.geological_setting: Columbite-tantalite occurs in lithium-caesium-tantalum (LCT) pegmatites — the same geological setting as spodumene. Canada's most significant resources include the Tanco Mine at Bernic Lake, Manitoba (historic tantalum producer, now operated for caesium), the Rose Lithium-Tantalum project in James Bay, Quebec, and the Separation Rapids deposit in Ontario. Niobium is also produced in large quantity from Quebec's Niobec mine at Saint-Honoré — one of the world's top niobium producers — though from carbonatite, not pegmatite.

handling: No significant toxicological hazards — both niobium and tantalum are biologically inert and used in medical implants. Apply standard mineral dust precautions. Note the supply chain context: columbite-tantalite (coltan) mined in conflict regions of the DRC is associated with documented human rights abuses. Canadian-sourced coltan is conflict-free.
known_for: The mineral that makes your electronics reliable. Tantalum capacitors are in virtually every smartphone, laptop, and medical device for their stability at high temperatures and small size. Niobium from columbite goes into high-strength pipeline and structural steels and into superalloys for jet engine turbine blades. Quebec's Niobec mine consistently ranks among the global top 3 niobium producers — making Canada a key supplier of this often-overlooked critical mineral.

name: Chalcopyrite

identification.colour: Brass-yellow, often with iridescent tarnish (peacock ore) of blue, purple, and green; streak: greenish-black

hardness.plain: Moderate — a copper coin will barely scratch it; softer than pyrite, which it superficially resembles

identification.crystal_habit: Disphenoidal (wedge-shaped) crystals; commonly massive and granular in ore; often intergrown with pyrite, bornite, and pyrrhotite

canada.geological_setting: Chalcopyrite is the world's most economically important copper mineral and occurs across virtually every sulphide deposit type in Canada. British Columbia's porphyry copper systems — Highland Valley (Canada's largest open-pit copper mine), Copper Mountain, Red Chris, and Gibraltar — host chalcopyrite as the primary ore mineral in disseminated and stockwork zones. Ontario's Sudbury Basin produces chalcopyrite as a co-product with pentlandite in magmatic nickel-copper deposits. The Flin Flon VMS camp (Manitoba-Saskatchewan) and Quebec's Abitibi Belt host chalcopyrite in ancient seafloor sulphide mounds. The Yukon's Whitehorse Copper Belt and Minto mine add additional porphyry resources.

handling: No significant toxicological hazard under normal handling. Chalcopyrite oxidises in air and water to produce sulphuric acid and copper sulphate — a process driving acid mine drainage in tailings. Do not ingest copper sulphate solution. Fine dust should not be inhaled (standard mineral precaution). Iridescent tarnished surfaces are caused by thin-film oxidation and are not a separate mineral.
known_for: The mineral that electrified the world. Copper from chalcopyrite is in every electrical wire, motor winding, EV battery connector, solar panel bus bar, and circuit board on earth. Canada is among the top 10 global copper producers, with British Columbia's porphyry deposits the backbone of domestic output. Chalcopyrite also concentrates gold — BC's porphyry mines recover significant gold as a by-product, often making gold revenue comparable to copper. The iridescent 'peacock ore' tarnish makes it one of the most visually striking minerals in any collection.
